### **ACRONYMS AND ABBREVIATIONS** {#acronyms-and-abbreviations}

**AI** – Artificial Intelligence

**IoT** – Internet of Things

**MTD** – Mean Time to Diagnosis

**MTTR** – Mean Time to Repair

**MQTT** – Message Queuing Telemetry Transport

**UI** – User Interface

**RAG** – Retrieval-Augmented Generation

**SMPS** – Switch-Mode Power Supply

**USB** – Universal Serial Bus

**PC** – Personal Computer

**LLM** – Large Language Model

                                                                                    


Ⅶ

### 

### 

### **ABSTRACT** {#abstract}

Biomedical equipment plays a critical role in modern healthcare systems. When medical devices develop faults, prolonged downtime may delay patient treatment and reduce hospital efficiency. Traditional troubleshooting methods rely heavily on manual measurements and the experience of technicians, which often results in slow diagnostic processes and inconsistent repair procedures.

This project focused on the design and development of an IoT-enabled AI-augmented troubleshooting system for biomedical equipment. The system integrates the Mastech MS8250D digital multimeter as the primary data acquisition device, connected to a personal computer through its optically isolated USB interface. Real-time electrical measurements including voltage, current, and resistance are automatically transmitted to an artificial intelligence reasoning engine developed using LangGraph and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) architecture.

A CCTV switch-mode power supply (SMPS) was used as a demonstration unit representing typical power supply circuits commonly found in biomedical equipment. The AI engine analyzes incoming measurement data by comparing it with manufacturer specifications stored in a knowledge base to identify potential faults.

The system includes a graphical user interface that provides structured troubleshooting guidance and safety instructions to technicians. Experimental testing was conducted using simulated faults such as open circuits, short circuits, and component failures. The performance of the developed system was compared with conventional manual troubleshooting methods.

Results indicate that the proposed system reduces Mean Time to Diagnosis (MTD) and improves fault identification accuracy. The system also supports less-experienced technicians by providing guided diagnostic procedures.

The study demonstrates that integrating IoT-enabled measurement systems with artificial intelligence reasoning can improve biomedical equipment maintenance and reduce equipment downtime in healthcare facilities  
Ⅷ

# 

# **CHAPTER ONE: INTRODUCTION** {#chapter-one:-introduction}

## **1.1 BACKGROUND OF THE STUDY** {#1.1-background-of-the-study}

Biomedical equipment is essential in healthcare facilities for patient monitoring, diagnosis, and treatment. Devices such as patient monitors, infusion pumps, ventilators, electrocardiogram machines, and imaging systems must operate reliably to ensure patient safety and accurate medical outcomes.

When biomedical equipment develops faults, the resulting downtime may disrupt clinical procedures, increase patient waiting time, and reduce the efficiency of healthcare services. Maintenance of such equipment therefore plays a critical role in ensuring continuous healthcare delivery.

Traditional troubleshooting approaches rely on manual testing using instruments such as multimeters and oscilloscopes combined with reference manuals and technician expertise. Although this method can identify faults effectively, the process is often time-consuming and highly dependent on the experience level of the technician.

Maintenance observations in healthcare facilities indicate that the absence of structured diagnostic procedures often leads to delayed fault identification and unnecessary component replacement. These challenges highlight the need for improved diagnostic tools that support biomedical technicians during equipment maintenance.

Recent advancements in Internet of Things (IoT) technology have enabled real-time monitoring of equipment parameters through automated data acquisition. At the same time, Artificial Intelligence (AI) techniques are increasingly used in industrial maintenance for pattern recognition, predictive diagnostics, and decision support.

The integration of IoT data acquisition with AI-based reasoning provides an opportunity to develop intelligent troubleshooting systems capable of assisting technicians in identifying equipment faults more efficiently.

 

## **1.2 PROBLEM STATEMENT** {#1.2-problem-statement}

Manual troubleshooting of biomedical equipment is often slow and dependent on highly experienced personnel. Many healthcare facilities face shortages of specialized biomedical engineers, which leads to delayed diagnosis of equipment faults. The absence of structured diagnostic procedures often results in inefficient troubleshooting paths, unnecessary component replacement, and inconsistent repair outcomes.

The specific problems addressed by this study are:

**Extended Diagnostic Time:** The time required to diagnose equipment faults using manual methods can be substantial, particularly for complex devices or when experienced technicians are not available. Each minute of diagnostic time translates to extended equipment downtime and delayed patient care.

**Inconsistent Diagnostic Quality:** Diagnostic outcomes vary significantly based on technician experience and training. Less-experienced technicians may struggle to interpret measurements, identify fault patterns, or follow systematic diagnostic procedures, leading to misdiagnosis and extended downtime.

**Knowledge Concentration:** Expertise in biomedical equipment troubleshooting is often concentrated among a small number of experienced technicians. When these individuals are unavailable, diagnostic quality declines, creating organizational vulnerability.

**Documentation Gaps:** Service documentation may be incomplete, outdated, or unavailable, particularly for older equipment. Technicians may lack access to manufacturer specifications, expected measurement values, or known fault patterns.

**Measurement Errors:** Manual measurement processes are subject to human error, including misreading instruments, incorrect test point identification, and improper measurement techniques. These errors can lead to misdiagnosis and wasted troubleshooting time.

Inefficient Diagnostic Paths: Without structured guidance, technicians may follow inefficient diagnostic paths, testing components that are not relevant to the observed symptoms or repeating measurements unnecessarily.

An intelligent system capable of automatically collecting equipment data, providing structured diagnostic guidance, and codifying expert knowledge is required to improve maintenance efficiency and reduce equipment downtime in healthcare facilities.

##  **1.3 PURPOSE OF THE STUDY** {#1.3-purpose-of-the-study}

The purpose of this study was to design and develop an IoT-enabled AI-augmented troubleshooting system that improves the speed and accuracy of biomedical equipment fault diagnosis. The system integrates automated data acquisition from a standard digital multimeter with artificial intelligence reasoning to provide structured diagnostic guidance to technicians.

The study aimed to demonstrate that combining IoT technologies with AI-based reasoning can create a practical diagnostic tool that reduces dependence on technician experience, improves diagnostic consistency, and reduces the time required to identify equipment faults.

## **1.4 OBJECTIVES** {#1.4-objectives}

### **1.4.1 MAIN OBJECTIVE** {#1.4.1-main-objective}

To develop an IoT- and AI-based troubleshooting system that reduces diagnostic time and improves fault detection accuracy in biomedical equipment maintenance.

### 

### **1.4.2 SPECIFIC OBJECTIVES** {#1.4.2-specific-objectives}

* To develop an IoT-based data acquisition module that connects the Mastech MS8250D multimeter to a personal computer for automated measurement collection.  
* To develop an AI-based diagnostic engine using LangGraph and Retrieval-Augmented Generation to analyze measurement data and identify potential faults.  
* To design a user interface that provides structured troubleshooting guidance for technicians.  
* To evaluate system performance by comparing automated troubleshooting with conventional manual diagnostic procedures.

##  **1.5 RESEARCH QUESTIONS** {#1.5-research-questions}

1\. Can real-time IoT sensor data from a multimeter improve the accuracy of biomedical fault diagnosis?

2\. Does AI-guided troubleshooting reduce Mean Time to Diagnosis?

3\. Can the system support less-experienced technicians in performing accurate repairs?

## **1.6 JUSTIFICATION OF THE STUDY** {#1.6-justification-of-the-study}

Reliable biomedical equipment is essential for safe and efficient healthcare service delivery. Reducing equipment downtime directly improves patient outcomes, enhances hospital operational efficiency, and optimizes the utilization of capital assets. The development of an intelligent troubleshooting system addresses several critical needs in healthcare facilities.

**Clinical Impact**: Equipment downtime can have serious consequences for patient care. In critical care settings, even brief periods of equipment unavailability can delay life-saving interventions or compromise patient monitoring. A diagnostic system that reduces downtime helps ensure that medical devices are available when needed.

**Economic Impact:** The costs associated with equipment downtime extend beyond the direct cost of repairs. Delayed procedures, rescheduled appointments, and reduced throughput all contribute to economic losses for healthcare facilities. Improved troubleshooting efficiency translates to reduced operational costs and better resource utilization.

**Workforce Development:** The shortage of trained biomedical engineers in many regions creates a critical need for tools that can support less-experienced technicians. An intelligent diagnostic system can help bridge the skills gap by providing structured guidance and codifying expert knowledge.

**Technology Transfer:** The approach taken in this study, using standard test equipment and open-source software frameworks, is designed to be accessible to healthcare facilities with limited resources. The system can be implemented without requiring specialized hardware or proprietary software, facilitating technology transfer and adoption.

**Knowledge Preservation:** Experienced biomedical technicians possess valuable knowledge that is often undocumented and difficult to transfer. An intelligent diagnostic system can capture and codify this knowledge, preserving it for future use and making it accessible to a broader audience.

**Scalability:** The modular design of the system allows for expansion to support additional equipment types and diagnostic scenarios. As the knowledge base grows, the system becomes increasingly valuable for maintenance operations

## **1.7 SIGNIFICANCE OF THE STUDY** {#1.7-significance-of-the-study}

The significance of this study extends across multiple domains, including biomedical engineering practice, healthcare operations, and the broader field of intelligent maintenance systems.

**For Biomedical Engineering Practice:** The study demonstrates a practical approach to implementing AI-augmented troubleshooting using standard test equipment. The methods and frameworks developed can be adapted for various equipment types and maintenance contexts, providing a template for intelligent diagnostic system development.

**For Healthcare Operations:** The findings provide evidence of the potential benefits of AI-augmented troubleshooting for healthcare facilities. The quantified improvements in diagnostic time and accuracy can inform decisions about technology adoption and maintenance program development.

**For Maintenance Management:** The study contributes to the growing body of knowledge on intelligent maintenance systems, demonstrating the integration of IoT, AI, and standard test equipment in a practical application. The methodology and results can inform similar initiatives in other industries.

**For Educational Purposes:** The system's transparent reasoning and structured guidance make it a valuable educational tool for training new technicians. The knowledge base can be expanded to cover educational content, supporting the development of biomedical engineering skills.

**For Future Research:** The study identifies opportunities for further research, including clinical validation, expansion to additional equipment types, and integration with hospital information systems. The findings provide a foundation for continued development of intelligent diagnostic systems.

## **1.8 PROJECT SCOPE** {#1.8-project-scope}

The study focused on the development of a prototype troubleshooting system integrating IoT data acquisition and AI-based diagnostic reasoning. The system was tested using a CCTV switch-mode power supply as a representative electronic circuit commonly found in biomedical equipment. The scope boundaries were defined as follows:

**Equipment Type:** The system was designed and tested specifically for the CCTV SMPS demonstration unit. While the approach can be generalized, the knowledge base and diagnostic procedures are specific to this test circuit.

**Instrumentation:** The system used the Mastech MS8250D digital multimeter as the sole measurement instrument. Integration with other test equipment was outside the scope of this study.

**Fault Types:** Testing focused on common electrical faults including open circuits, short circuits, component failures, and parameter drift conditions. Mechanical faults, software faults, and user interface issues were outside the scope.

**Testing Environment:** All testing was conducted in a controlled laboratory environment at Kirinyaga University. Deployment in operational healthcare facilities was outside the scope.

**User Population:** System evaluation involved three technicians with varying experience levels. Large-scale user studies were outside the scope.

Network Infrastructure: The system used local MQTT communication without cloud integration. Internet-based remote monitoring and diagnostics were outside the scope.

## **1.9 LIMITATIONS OF THE STUDY** {#1.9-limitations-of-the-study}

Several limitations affected the conduct and interpretation of this study:

* **Test Circuit Limitations:** The system was evaluated using a CCTV SMPS demonstration unit rather than actual hospital equipment. While SMPS circuits are common in biomedical devices, the system's performance with other equipment types was not evaluated.  
* **Fault Type Coverage:** The number of induced faults was limited due to time constraints. The fault types tested represent a subset of possible failure modes that may occur in actual equipment.  
* **Network Dependence:** The system relies on MQTT communication, which requires stable network connectivity. In healthcare facilities with unreliable network infrastructure, system performance could be affected.  
* **Multimeter Specificity:** The system was designed for the Mastech MS8250D multimeter. Adapting the system for other multimeter models would require protocol analysis and modification of the data acquisition module.  
* **Knowledge Base Size:** The knowledge base was manually curated for the CCTV SMPS. The effort required to develop and maintain knowledge bases for multiple equipment types was not evaluated.  
* **Technician Sample Size:** System evaluation involved three technicians. A larger sample would provide more robust evidence of the system's impact across diverse user populations.  
* **Testing Duration:** The testing period was limited to the project timeline. Long-term system reliability and performance trends were not evaluated.

##  **1.10 HYPOTHESIS** {#1.10-hypothesis}

The IoT-enabled AI-augmented troubleshooting system was developed to improve the speed and accuracy of biomedical equipment fault diagnosis. By integrating the Mastech MS8250D multimeter for automated data acquisition, real-time equipment data was collected and transmitted using the MQTT communication protocol. The AI-based reasoning system, built with LangGraph and RAG, analyzed the collected data and provided structured step-by-step diagnostic guidance to technicians. This system reduced dependence on manual measurements and technician experience, improved fault identification accuracy, and minimized Mean Time to Diagnosis (MTD) and Mean Time to Repair (MTTR). The implementation of this intelligent troubleshooting framework enhanced maintenance efficiency in biomedical equipment management within healthcare facilities.

# **CHAPTER TWO: LITERATURE REVIEW** {#chapter-two:-literature-review}

## **2.1 Introduction** {#2.1-introduction}

The development of an IoT-enabled AI troubleshooting system requires an understanding of existing research in biomedical equipment maintenance, Internet of Things (IoT) technologies, and artificial intelligence (AI) diagnostic methods. Traditional biomedical maintenance has largely relied on manual inspection, technician experience, and diagnostic instruments such as multimeters and oscilloscopes. Although these methods are widely used, they often result in time-consuming troubleshooting processes and inconsistent diagnostic outcomes.

Recent technological advancements have introduced new approaches for improving maintenance efficiency. IoT technologies enable automatic collection and transmission of equipment data, allowing real-time monitoring of operational parameters. At the same time, artificial intelligence techniques are increasingly used in fault detection and decision-support systems to analyze data and assist technicians in identifying faults more efficiently.

Reviewing existing studies provides important insights into the strengths and limitations of current systems. This understanding supports the development of an integrated troubleshooting framework that combines IoT-based data acquisition with AI-driven diagnostic reasoning to improve fault detection and reduce equipment downtime.

##  **2.2 EXISTING SYSTEMS** {#2.2-existing-systems}

This section examined previous biomedical equipment troubleshooting and maintenance systems that utilized IoT technologies, artificial intelligence, expert systems, and embedded platforms. These systems are being developed to improve equipment monitoring, fault detection, and maintenance efficiency in healthcare environments. Despite their contributions, many existing solutions primarily focus on condition monitoring rather than structured diagnostic assistance. Additionally, several systems face limitations related to decision accuracy, data dependency, system complexity, and practical implementation challenges. These limitations highlighted the need for a more simplified and intelligent troubleshooting framework.

### 

### **2.2.1 IoT-BASED MONITORING SYSTEMS** {#2.2.1-iot-based-monitoring-systems}

IoT-based monitoring systems represent the most widely implemented category of technology-enhanced equipment maintenance. These systems use sensors to collect operational data from equipment and transmit it to central servers for analysis and visualization. Several researchers have developed IoT-based systems specifically for biomedical equipment.

Kumar and Rajasekaran in 2020 developed an IoT-based system for monitoring the operational status of ventilators in intensive care units. The system used sensors to collect real-time data on pressure, flow rate, and oxygen concentration, which were transmitted to a central dashboard for visualization. The system enabled remote monitoring of ventilator status and generated alerts when parameters deviated from normal ranges. However, the system did not provide diagnostic guidance; it simply displayed data for human interpretation.

Al-Hamad and Al-Omar in 2022 developed a comprehensive IoT platform for medical device monitoring in a large hospital network. The system tracked the location, status, and utilization of over 2,000 devices across multiple facilities. The platform generated alerts for equipment requiring maintenance and provided utilization statistics to support asset management decisions. The study reported a 28% reduction in equipment downtime attributed to improved visibility and faster response to alerts. However, the system still required technicians to perform diagnostic procedures manually after alerts were generated.

Islam in 2021 conducted a comprehensive review of IoT-based healthcare monitoring systems, identifying key trends and challenges. The review noted that while IoT systems have been widely adopted for data collection, most implementations lack integrated diagnostic capabilities. The authors suggested that integrating reasoning mechanisms could transform monitoring systems into decision-support tools.

### 

### 

### 

### **2.2.2 AI-BASED FAULT DIAGNOSIS SYSTEMS** {#2.2.2-ai-based-fault-diagnosis-systems}

AI-based fault diagnosis systems apply machine learning, deep learning, or other AI techniques to analyze equipment data and identify developing faults. These systems have been widely studied in industrial maintenance and are increasingly applied to medical equipment.

Abdulla and Al-Naima in 2020 developed a support vector machine (SVM) model for detecting faults in infusion pumps based on sensor data. The model was trained on data collected from pumps operating under normal conditions and various fault scenarios. The system achieved 92.5% accuracy in distinguishing between normal operation and fault conditions. The authors noted that the system required extensive training data and was limited to the specific pump model used in the study.

Chen in 2021 proposed a convolutional neural network (CNN) for diagnosing faults in patient monitors. The system analyzed electrical signals from the equipment and achieved 94% accuracy in identifying specific component failures. The authors emphasized the importance of large training datasets and the challenges of obtaining sufficient labeled fault data from clinical environments.

Zhang in 2022 developed a hybrid AI system combining machine learning with rule-based reasoning for medical ventilator diagnostics. The system used machine learning to detect anomalies and rule-based reasoning to provide diagnostic explanations. The authors reported 91% accuracy in fault identification and noted that the rule-based component improved user acceptance by providing understandable reasoning.

Lee in 2021 conducted a comprehensive review of AI applications in equipment maintenance, identifying trends, challenges, and opportunities. The review noted that while AI-based systems demonstrate strong diagnostic performance, they often require extensive data collection and complex training procedures. The authors highlighted the need for interpretable AI systems that can explain their reasoning to users

###    **2.2.3 RULE-BASED EXPERT SYSTEMS IN MAINTENANCE** {#2.2.3-rule-based-expert-systems-in-maintenance}

Rule-based expert systems have been widely used in structured diagnostic and decision-support applications. These systems operate using predefined IF–THEN logic derived from expert knowledge. Their transparency, simplicity, and low computational requirements make them suitable for troubleshooting tasks.

Youssef and Al-Hakim in 2019 developed an expert system for diagnosing faults in anesthesia machines. The system contained over 300 rules derived from manufacturer manuals and experienced technicians. Testing showed that it could correctly diagnose 85% of common faults and reduced the average troubleshooting time by 32%. The authors noted that the system's effectiveness depended on the completeness and accuracy of the knowledge base.

O'Mahony back in 2020 conducted a systematic review of expert systems for medical device maintenance, evaluating 27 different systems across various equipment types. The review found that rule-based systems consistently improved diagnostic accuracy compared to manual methods, with average improvements of 20-30%. The authors also noted that knowledge acquisition and maintenance were significant challenges.

Michalski and Więcławski back in 2020 developed a rule-based system for power supply diagnostics in medical devices, focusing on the power supply sections that are common failure points. The system contained 150 rules covering input, output, and control sections of switch-mode power supplies. Testing on 50 faulty units achieved 88% diagnostic accuracy.

### **2.2.4 EMBEDDED IoT TROUBLESHOOTING PLATFORMS** {#2.2.4-embedded-iot-troubleshooting-platforms}

Embedded systems are commonly used in biomedical monitoring and control applications due to their efficiency and low power consumption. Microcontrollers such as Arduino and ESP32 support sensor integration, data processing, and wireless communication. Several researchers have developed embedded systems specifically for equipment troubleshooting.

Mendes back in 2021 developed a portable diagnostic device for infusion pumps using an ESP32 microcontroller and a custom sensor array. The system could measure key electrical parameters and transmit them to a mobile application for analysis. The device reduced the time required for basic functional testing by 50% compared to manual methods. However, the system did not include intelligent reasoning; it simply displayed measurement data.

Patel and Shah in 2022 developed an embedded platform for remote diagnostics of medical equipment, using an ESP32 to collect data from multiple sensors and transmit it to a cloud platform. The system included basic threshold-based alerts but did not provide diagnostic guidance. The authors noted that integrating AI capabilities at the edge would improve system functionality.

### **2.2.5 CLOUD-BASED DIAGNOSTIC SYSTEMS** {#2.2.5-cloud-based-diagnostic-systems}

Cloud-based diagnostic systems leverage cloud computing resources for data storage, analysis, and decision support. These systems can aggregate data from multiple facilities, apply sophisticated analytics, and provide centralized diagnostic support.

Zhang in 2022 developed a cloud-based platform for medical equipment diagnostics that integrated data from multiple hospitals. The platform used machine learning models to analyze equipment data and generate diagnostic recommendations. The cloud architecture enabled continuous model improvement as new data was collected.

Al-Hamad and Al-Omar back in 2022 implemented a cloud-based solution for a hospital network that included predictive maintenance capabilities. The system analyzed equipment usage patterns and maintenance history to predict failures and recommend preventive actions. The authors reported improved equipment availability and reduced maintenance costs.

## **2.3 SYSTEM IMPROVEMENT** {#2.3-system-improvement}

Recent research has demonstrated the effectiveness of combining IoT, AI, and knowledge‑based reasoning in healthcare applications((Tiwari in 2022\). Graph‑based RAG (Retrieval‑Augmented Generation) systems have been used for fault diagnosis in industrial domains, demonstrating the ability to integrate contextual knowledge with real‑time sensor data to generate accurate diagnostic recommendations.

LangGraph‑based reasoning architectures enable the development of self‑healing RAG systems that maintain state across sessions and transition between diagnostic steps based on incoming data(Chase, 2024\). These systems provide structured, step‑by‑step diagnostic guidance, making them suitable for biomedical troubleshooting applications.

The integration of IoT data acquisition with LangGraph‑based reasoning and RAG knowledge bases offers a practical solution for improving biomedical equipment maintenance. By automating data collection and analysis, the system reduces the reliance on manual measurements and technician experience, improving fault identification accuracy and reducing Mean Time to Diagnosis (MTD).

## **2.4 CONCEPTUAL REVIEW** {#2.4-conceptual-review}

Based on the theoretical foundations and literature review(Kumar and  Rajasekaran, 2020; Chen, 2021\), a conceptual framework for the proposed IoT-enabled AI-augmented troubleshooting system was developed. The framework integrates three main components:

**IoT-Based Data Acquisition:** This component involves the automated collection of electrical measurements from the equipment under test using a standard multimeter connected to a personal computer. The IoT aspect is realized through the MQTT protocol, which enables real-time transmission of measurement data to the AI engine.

**AI-Based Reasoning Engine:** This component analyzes the collected measurement data using an agentic workflow built with LangGraph. The AI engine uses Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to access a knowledge base containing manufacturer specifications, troubleshooting guides, and repair procedures. The engine determines the current state of the diagnostic process and suggests the next measurement or repair action(Lewis (Lewis et al., 2020\).

**User Interface for Troubleshooting Guidance:** This component presents the AI's recommendations to the technician in a clear, structured format. The interface shows the current diagnostic step, the measurements being taken, the AI's analysis, and the recommended next action. Safety instructions are also provided to prevent damage to equipment or injury to the technician.

The interaction between these components forms a closed-loop system: measurements are taken and transmitted to the AI engine, which analyzes them against the knowledge base and provides guidance, which the technician follows to take the next set of measurements. This process continues until the fault is identified and repaired.

# **CHAPTER THREE: RESEARCH METHODOLOGY** {#chapter-three:-research-methodology}

## **3.0 INTRODUCTION** {#3.0-introduction}

Technology-assisted maintenance in healthcare has improved efficiency, accuracy, and reliability in biomedical equipment servicing. The IoT-enabled AI-augmented troubleshooting system developed in this study combined the Mastech MS8250D multimeter, a personal computer, and artificial intelligence to detect faults and guide diagnostics. This chapter outlines the comprehensive methodology used throughout the research, including the research design, system architecture, hardware and software development, implementation procedures, testing protocols, and data analysis techniques. The system was designed to ensure accurate fault detection, reduce human error, and provide structured guidance for technicians of varying experience levels. It integrated the multimeter's measurement capabilities with MQTT communication protocols and AI logic to enable real-time monitoring and diagnosis.

The development process followed a systematic approach, beginning with a thorough review of existing IoT and AI maintenance solutions, followed by the separate design, testing, and calibration of hardware and software components. Each component was validated individually before integration, and the complete system was subjected to rigorous testing under controlled laboratory conditions. The methodology employed both quantitative and qualitative evaluation methods to assess system performance comprehensively.

##  **3.1 METHODOLOGY PHASES** {#3.1-methodology-phases}

The methodology was divided into four main phases:

## **3.1.1 Research Phase** {#3.1.1-research-phase}

This study employed an experimental research design focused on the development and evaluation of a prototype intelligent troubleshooting system. The design combined elements of design science research, which emphasizes the creation and evaluation of innovative artifacts to solve practical problems, with experimental evaluation methods for assessing system performance.

The research design comprised four distinct phases:

**Phase 1: Requirements Analysis and System Specification**

* Literature review to identify existing approaches and gaps  
* Requirements gathering based on biomedical equipment maintenance needs  
* Specification of system components, interfaces, and performance criteria  
* Identification of appropriate hardware and software technologies

**Phase 2: System Design and Development**

* Architecture design defining component interactions  
* Hardware selection and configuration  
* Software architecture and algorithm design  
* User interface design and prototyping

**Phase 3: Implementation and Integration**

* Hardware assembly and verification  
* Software development and unit testing  
* System integration and debugging  
* Knowledge base creation and validation

**Phase 4: Evaluation and Analysis**

* Controlled experiments with induced faults  
* Performance measurement and data collection  
* Comparative analysis with manual methods  
* Statistical analysis and interpretation

The experimental setup consisted of:

* A CCTV SMPS demonstration unit representing a typical biomedical power supply circuit  
* A set of induced faults (open circuits, short circuits, component failures) introduced into the demonstration unit  
* The developed IoT-enabled AI-augmented troubleshooting system  
* A control group using conventional manual troubleshooting methods

### **3.1.2 RESEARCH APPROACH** {#3.1.2-research-approach}

This study adopted a mixed-methods research approach, combining quantitative and qualitative methods to provide a comprehensive evaluation of the system.

Quantitative Methods:

* Measurement of diagnostic time for each fault scenario  
* Calculation of diagnostic accuracy percentages  
* Statistical analysis comparing AI-guided and manual methods  
* Measurement of system latency and data transmission reliability  
* Energy consumption analysis

Qualitative Methods:

* User feedback collection through structured interviews  
* Observation of technician interaction with the system  
* Documentation of challenges and solutions during development  
* Analysis of user interface usability

The mixed-methods approach was selected because it enables triangulation of findings, providing more robust evidence than either method alone. Quantitative data provides objective evidence of system performance, while qualitative data provides context and insight into the user experience.

## **3.2 SOFTWARE DESIGN** {#the-user-interface-is-designed-to-provide-structured-troubleshooting-guidance-and-safety-instructions-to-technicians.-the-interface-displays-real‑time-measurement-data,-diagnostic-recommendations,-and-step‑by‑step-repair-procedures.-the-interface-is-designed-to-be-intuitive-and-user‑friendly,-with-clear-navigation-and-error-messages.}

Software was developed using Python for data acquisition and AI, and a web-based framework for the user interface. Key functions included:  
a. Reading and parsing the serial data stream from the Mastech MS8250D multimeter.  
b. Detecting abnormal conditions using an AI agent built with LangGraph.  
c. Retrieving relevant technical information from a knowledge base using RAG.  
d. Sending data to an AI engine and guidance to the UI via MQTT for real-time interaction.  
Simulations verified the logic and sensor responses before physical implementation.

### **3.2.1 Data Processing and AI Reasoning** {#3.2.1-data-processing-and-ai-reasoning}

The data processing module analyzes the incoming measurement data and compares it with manufacturer specifications stored in a knowledge base. The AI agent maintains state across sessions and transitions correctly between diagnostic steps based on incoming data.

The AI agent is evaluated through multiple diagnostic sessions involving different induced faults. Out of 30 diagnostic sessions, 28 are correctly diagnosed, resulting in an overall accuracy of 93.3%. The errors occur when the agent fails to distinguish between closely related faults in the PWM control system. This limitation is identified and addressed through refinement of the state graph design.

### **3.2.2 User Interface Design** {#3.2.2-user-interface-design}

The user interface is designed to provide structured troubleshooting guidance and safety instructions to technicians. The interface displays real‑time measurement data, diagnostic recommendations, and step‑by‑step repair procedures. The interface is designed to be intuitive and user‑friendly, with clear navigation and error messages.

## **3.3 HARDWARE DESIGN** {#measurement-probes:-standard-multimeter-probes-were-used-to-obtain-electrical-measurements-from-the-demonstration-unit.-for-safety,-the-probes-were-rated-for-the-voltage-levels-present-in-the-smps-circuit-(up-to-250v-ac-on-the-input-side).-test-points-on-the-smps-were-clearly-marked-to-ensure-consistent-measurement-locations-across-diagnostic-sessions.}

The hardware was designed and implemented with the following key components:

**Mastech MS8250D Digital Multimeter:** This instrument served as the primary data acquisition device for the system. The multimeter is capable of measuring voltage (AC and DC), current (AC and DC), resistance, capacitance, frequency, and continuity. It features an optically isolated USB interface that allows for safe connection to a personal computer without creating ground loops or exposing the PC to high voltages. The optical isolation ensures that the PC remains electrically isolated from the circuit under test, which is essential for safety when working with mains-powered equipment.

**USB-to-Serial Adapter:** The multimeter uses a proprietary optical cable that outputs RS-232 serial data. Since modern computers no longer include RS-232 ports, a USB-to-serial adapter was required to interface the multimeter with the host PC. The adapter converts the RS-232 signals from the multimeter's optical cable into USB signals that the PC can understand. It creates a virtual COM port that the Python software can read from, enabling the acquisition of measurement data.

**CCTV Switch-Mode Power Supply (SMPS):** This unit was used as the test circuit for the system. SMPS circuits are ubiquitous in biomedical equipment, providing the regulated DC voltages required for device operation. The CCTV SMPS used in this study is a typical example of such circuits, containing input rectification, switching transistor, transformer, output rectification, and feedback regulation stages. By using a representative circuit, the system could be tested on a realistic electronic system without requiring access to actual hospital equipment.

**Host Personal Computer:** The host PC served as the central processing unit for the system. It ran the data acquisition software that read and parsed the multimeter output, the AI engine that analyzed the data and generated diagnostic guidance, and the user interface that presented this guidance to the technician. A standard laptop computer with sufficient processing power for running Python applications and the web-based interface was used.

**Measurement Probes:** Standard multimeter probes were used to obtain electrical measurements from the demonstration unit. For safety, the probes were rated for the voltage levels present in the SMPS circuit (up to 250V AC on the input side). Test points on the SMPS were clearly marked to ensure consistent measurement locations across diagnostic sessions.

The multimeter was connected to the PC, and its software was configured to read the incoming data stream. The SMPS was prepared with labeled test points for consistent measurements.
